claims.text: The Casimir force per unit area between two perfectly conducting, infinite, parallel plates separated by distance d is F/A = -π²ℏc/(240 d⁴)
The negative sign indicates an attractive force.
The plates are assumed to be infinite and located at z=0 and z=d.
For a perfectly conducting surface the tangential components of the electric field must vanish.
The magnetic field normal to the surface vanishes for a perfectly conducting surface.
The allowed modes in the z direction are quantized with mode functions proportional to sin(nπz/d).
k_z = nπ/d, with n = 1, 2, 3, ...
In the x and y directions the wave-vectors k_x and k_y are continuous because the plates are infinite.
There is a subtlety regarding the n=0 mode and how the two physical polarizations are counted, but these details do not affect the final result.
claims.notes: n=0 contributes for one polarization (TM); claim it doesn't affect result needs justification.

claims.text: The dispersion relation is ω_n(k_⊥) = c√(k_⊥² + (nπ/d)²).
The vacuum (zero-point) energy is obtained by summing ½ℏω for every mode.
The energy per unit area is U(d)/A = (ℏ/2) Σ_modes ω.
claims.notes: Missing factor of 2 for polarizations in this expression.
Missing factor of 2 for two polarizations.

claims.text: This sum/integral is divergent.
A physically motivated regularization is to introduce an exponential cutoff factor e^{-λω} with λ > 0.
The regularized energy is U(d,λ)/A = (ℏ/2) Σ_{n=1}^∞ ∫ d²k_⊥/(2π)² c√(k_⊥²+(nπ/d)²) e^{-λc√(k_⊥²+(nπ/d)²)}
ζ(-3) = 1/120, where ζ(s) is the Riemann zeta function.
Converting the k_⊥ integration to polar coordinates gives d²k_⊥ = 2π k dk.
Substituting ω = √(k²+(nπ/d)²), when k runs from 0 to ∞, ω runs from nπ/d to ∞.
Under this substitution, k dk = ω dω.
U(d,λ)/A = (ℏc)/(4π) Σ_{n=1}^∞ ∫_{nπ/d}^∞ dω ω² e^{-λcω}
Substituting x = λcω transforms the integral to (1/(λc)³) ∫_{λcnπ/d}^∞ x² e^{-x} dx.
U(d,λ)/A = (ℏc)/(4π(λc)³) Σ_{n=1}^∞ e^{-λc(nπ/d)} [(λcnπ/d)² + 2λc(nπ/d) + 2]
The regulated energy still contains divergences as λ→0.
These divergences are independent of the plate separation d and are present also in free space.
The finite Casimir energy per unit area is defined as U_Casimir(d)/A = U(d)/A - U(d→∞)/A.
Only the d-dependent part is physically observable (in the form of a force).
After subtracting the d-independent divergent terms, one obtains U_Casimir(d)/A = -π²ℏc/(720 d³).
The summation is recast in terms of Riemann zeta functions; one encounters sums of the form Σ_{n=1}^∞ n³.
claims.notes: Intermediate steps showing how Σn³ arises are skipped.

claims.text: Σ_{n=1}^∞ n³ is assigned a finite value by analytic continuation using ζ(-3) = 1/120.
The Casimir force per unit area is obtained by F(d)/A = -∂/∂d (U_Casimir(d)/A).
The derivative of -π²ℏc/(720 d³) with respect to d is -π²ℏc/(720) · (-3)/d⁴ = π²ℏc/(240 d⁴).
F(d)/A = -π²ℏc/(240 d⁴)
The plates are assumed to be perfectly conducting and infinite in extent.
The electromagnetic field is quantized in the region between the plates with boundary conditions that force the tangential electric field to vanish at the plate surfaces.
The zero-point energy per mode is taken to be ½ℏω.
An exponential cutoff factor e^{-λω} with subsequent removal via λ→0 was used to regularize the divergent sums.
The subtraction of the free-vacuum (d→∞) contribution isolates the physically observable, d-dependent piece.
In evaluating divergent sums such as Σn³, the analytic continuation of the Riemann zeta function (ζ(-3)=1/120) is employed.
